explanation: The subject displays a Stage 2 Instrumental Mind[cite: 1]. Their worldview centers on transactional relationships, self-interest, and avoiding negative consequences[cite: 1]. They view rules as 'obstacles you have to navigate so you don't get kicked out' and evaluate relationships based on utility, such as making a good impression so others 'can help me later'[cite: 1].

The subject operates at Stage 3, the Socialized Mind[cite: 1]. Their self-worth is entirely tied to external validation, belonging, and interpersonal harmony[cite: 1]. They express 'worrying way too much about whether the other teachers think I'm doing a good job' and feel 'the pressure to always live up to everyone else's expectations'[cite: 1].

This candidate exhibits a classic Stage 3 framework[cite: 1]. Their identity is defined by their relationships and their ability to please others[cite: 1]. They note that their main problem is 'trying to please everyone all the time' and they judge their own success by whether they are 'making her family proud'[cite: 1].

The subject demonstrates a heavily institutionalized Stage 3 mindset[cite: 1]. Their sense of self is anchored in external respect, competence in the eyes of authority, and maintaining appearances[cite: 1]. They worry about losing their boss's trust and state that education is important for 'getting respected in your career'[cite: 1].

This subject shows a strong Stage 3 orientation within a team or military context[cite: 1]. Their loyalty is absolute to their group, noting 'your squad is everything'[cite: 1]. They experience conflict when external expectations clash, feeling 'stuck in the middle trying to figure out how to keep everyone happy'[cite: 1].

The subject operates at Stage 4, the Self-Authoring Mind[cite: 1]. They evaluate external feedback through an internal value system, stating they 'don't let it define my self-worth'[cite: 1]. They recognize the difference between societal expectations and their own authentic choices, valuing their 'ability to think for myself and not just follow the crowd'[cite: 1].

This candidate displays a solid Stage 4 structure[cite: 1]. They can treat their own emotions and initial defensiveness as 'data' or an 'object' to be analyzed rather than being consumed by them[cite: 1]. They value relationships built on 'mutual respect, clear boundaries, and self-awareness' rather than co-dependency[cite: 1].

The subject exhibits a Stage 4 systemic mindset[cite: 1]. They take complete ownership of their outcomes and focus on optimizing systems and boundaries[cite: 1]. They identify themselves as 'the author of my own life' and recognize when rules 'no longer serve their purpose' and should be revised[cite: 1].

This candidate demonstrates Stage 4 characteristics by prioritizing an internal compass and self-determined principles[cite: 1]. They view relationships as partnerships of distinct individuals, striving to 'support their spouse's individual growth' while maintaining their 'own independent space'[cite: 1].

The subject is anchored in Stage 4, focusing heavily on internal integrity and principled leadership[cite: 1]. They define success not by external validation but by knowing 'they have done the right thing'[cite: 1]. They view rules as guidelines that 'must sometimes be interpreted or overridden by a well-reasoned internal compass'[cite: 1].

The subject operates at Stage 5, the Self-Transforming Mind[cite: 1]. They view identity, truth, and relationships as fluid, systemic processes rather than static realities[cite: 1]. They actively seek friction to grow and express a 'willingness to let my deepest convictions be challenged and unmade'[cite: 1].

This candidate exemplifies Stage 5 by deeply valuing interconnectedness and the co-creation of reality[cite: 1]. They view boundaries as permeable, discovering 'the fluidity of myself, as we subtly alter each other in every interaction'[cite: 1]. They remain open to the idea that their 'most closely held beliefs might need to evolve'[cite: 1].

The subject is in the 2/3 transitional stage[cite: 1]. They are moving from an instrumental focus to a rule-bound, socialized identity within a tight-knit group[cite: 1]. While they deeply value 'brotherhood' and 'loyalty,' their morality is still tied to concrete group codes, noting they might bend rules for a buddy but would 'never lie' to cover it up[cite: 1].

This subject is in the 3/4 transitional stage, actively asserting self-authorship against socialized expectations[cite: 1]. They express a 'refusal to just go along with what people expect,' yet this independence is often framed in opposition to others, showing they are still working to fully separate their identity from external pressures[cite: 1].

The candidate is in the 3/4 transition, demonstrating tension between establishing an independent worldview and desiring external approval[cite: 1]. They are proud to be 'finally starting to think for myself' but acknowledge the struggle of 'balancing my need for independence with my desire for my family to accept my choices'[cite: 1].

This subject represents a 3/4 transition[cite: 1]. They recognize the need for a self-authored voice but are frequently hindered by Stage 3 fears of judgment[cite: 1]. They note they are 'learning to trust my own voice and expertise, even when it is scary' but also admit to 'worrying way too much about what my principal or peers think'[cite: 1].

The candidate is transitioning from Stage 3 to 4, exhibiting a strong drive for autonomy that sometimes presents as rigid defensiveness[cite: 1]. They value being 'the one who makes the final call' but recognize their tendency to get 'overly defensive and pushing back hard' when their authority is questioned by superiors[cite: 1].

This subject is in the 3/4 transition, building a self-authored moral framework while consciously unlearning Stage 3 habits[cite: 1]. They note a 'growing ability to do the right thing even when nobody is watching' but still occasionally catch themselves doing things 'just for the praise' instead of true conviction[cite: 1].

The candidate is in the 3/4 transitional stage, possessing the self-awareness of Stage 4 but the emotional reflexes of Stage 3[cite: 1]. They know they need to enforce boundaries but admit they 'freeze because I'm terrified of being disliked,' highlighting the friction between their internal knowledge and their socialized fears[cite: 1].

This subject is navigating the classic 3/4 cross-categorical conflict[cite: 1]. They are caught between competing external loyalties—work and family—without a self-authored system to prioritize them[cite: 1]. They feel 'completely exhausted from trying to keep all the plates spinning' and 'can't bear' to disappoint either side[cite: 1].

The subject is transitioning from Stage 4 to 5[cite: 1]. They possess a strong self-authored leadership capacity but increasingly recognize the limitations of a single, fixed perspective[cite: 1]. They are actively trying to 'loosen my grip on the ideologies I've built' while balancing the practical 'need to act decisively in leadership'[cite: 1].

This candidate is in the 4/5 transition, displaying comfort with ambiguity and a desire to deconstruct their own frameworks[cite: 1]. They suppress the 'instinct to defend myself' to find data that exposes their blind spots, and they are learning to value 'the questions that disrupt my models just as much as the models themselves'[cite: 1].

The subject is moving into Stage 5, heavily emphasizing the incomplete nature of their own worldview[cite: 1]. They no longer feel the need to 'defend the fortress of my ideas' and view models as temporary[cite: 1]. They are comfortable with the 'irreducible complexity' of life and find joy in dismantling their own life's work[cite: 1].
